title: 3.1 and 3.2 practice
description: Practice what you learned in lesson 3.1 and 3.2!
topic_path: Early Agrarian Societies (6000 BCE to 100 CE)

assessment_items.question_content:
**Nomadic societies played an important role in early trade networks. Which of the following statements best explains that role?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Trade between farming villages and nomadic societies expanded trade networks.
Trade between farming villages and nomadic societies minimized trade networks.
Trade between farming villages and nomadic societies increased conflict along trade routes.
Trade between farming villages and nomadic societies decreased conflict along trade routes.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes examples of non-agricultural societies in early history and how they functioned?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Pastoralist and foraging groups moved regularly and often relied on trade or seasonal resources to survive.
Non-agricultural societies lived in permanent cities and relied on imported grain to support large populations.
Most non-agricultural societies built large-scale irrigation systems to support their herds.
Pastoralists and foragers typically avoided forming social or trade relationships with agricultural villages.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best explains what conditions allowed early humans to settle in one place for long periods of time?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: a reliable food source
favorable weather patterns
natural protections
access to trade routes

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes how trade influenced social organization in early farming communities?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Trade expanded social hierarchies.
Trade equalized social status.
Trade minimized the concept of private wealth.
Trade weakened family structures.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best explains how trade developed in early village communities over time?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Trade began with exchanges of surplus goods and gradually expanded into wider networks.
Early villages avoided trade, relying only on what they could produce themselves.
Trade was immediately organized by governments and required written records.
Long-distance trade networks developed rapidly alongside the formation of early villages.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best explains how trade and exchange networks transformed life in early villages and cities?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They promoted specialization and deepened social hierarchies.
They caused most early cities to abandon agriculture in favor of full-time trading.
They discouraged interaction between distant communities, making cities more isolated.
They led to less defined social roles by encouraging equal access to resources.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did patterns of growth vary among early farming villages?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Differences in environment, trade access, and resources led communities to grow in distinct ways.
All farming villages expanded at the same pace due to the shared benefits of agriculture.
Early farming villages grew uniformly because they copied their structures from nomadic camps.
Farming villages avoided change, choosing to stay the same across generations.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best explains the claim that early villages were largely *egalitarian*?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Resources and roles were shared equally among members, with little difference in status or wealth.
Religious leaders held most of the power and controlled how food and tools were distributed.
Each family lived completely independently and rarely interacted with others in the village.
Strict class systems determined who could own land and who performed manual labor.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best explains how historians determine social status in early village communities based on available evidence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: By examining differences in burial sites and the goods buried with individuals.
By reading government records that list citizens by class.
By studying ancient novels that describe daily village life.
By tracking modern family lineages back to early farming societies.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What role did animals play in the daily lives and survival of nomadic groups?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Animals were central to transportation, food, and trade in nomadic societies.
Animals were rarely used by nomadic groups due to their constant movement.
Animals were primarily used for entertainment and ceremonial purposes.
Animals replaced the need for trade with settled farming societies.

assessment_items.question_content:
>When drawing the calculations and plans, the males (men) defer to the opinion of females (women) and settle on their own only the military affairs.


**Based on the observation above, what does this suggest about the role of women in some nomadic societies?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Women held significant influence in planning and decision-making.
Women were excluded from most aspects of leadership.
Women were responsible only for domestic and child-rearing tasks.
Women led military campaigns and organized defenses.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What role did pastoral nomads play in the growth of the Silk Road trade network?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They facilitated trade by guiding caravans and connecting distant regions.
They avoided trade entirely and stayed isolated from economic exchange.
They primarily served as military forces protecting cities along the route.
They disrupted trade by attacking caravans and seizing goods.
